Weakness: The options do not address cost and timeline implications of retrofitting existing suites or the potential for differential treatment effects across suite types that could confound longitudinal analysis.

levers.name: Mid-Stay Participant Logistics and Cumulative Attrition Risk
levers.consequences: Participants enrolled for 2–8 weeks (with potential 2-week extensions) face mounting fatigue, disruption to work and family, and likelihood of protocol departure or poor-quality final-week sleep. Early discharge of low-yield participants frees capacity but introduces selection bias; retention incentives and flexible scheduling reduce bias but increase per-participant cost and coordinatorial overhead.
options: Implement a rolling two-week intake model with staggered start dates (two participants starting each Monday), allowing automatic staff rotation and kitchen-operation predictability, and establish a hard discharge boundary at week 6 regardless of event yield, with optional post-hoc outpatient follow-up actigraphy for participants willing to continue in non-residential format.

Offer mid-stay flexibility options—weekend pass privileges, compressed sleep schedules (e.g., intentional nap periods during low-monitoring windows), or remote work accommodation within the facility's common areas—bundled with €40 mid-stay bonus conditional on retention through week 6 and active engagement in scheduled enhanced nights.

Partner with local hospitality providers (Airbnb hosts, serviced apartments) to create a hybrid second-half program: participants completing week 4 with adequate event capture transition to nearby residential accommodation with take-home actigraphy, reducing facility occupancy pressure while maintaining participant engagement and reducing attrition among high-capture-yield individuals.
levers.review: Controls trade-off between stay length (required for adequate event capture) and participant retention (threatened by fatigue and life disruption). Weakness: The options do not quantify the attrition risk thresholds or define what constitutes 'adequate event capture' at each milestone, potentially leading to inconsistent discharge decisions.

levers.name: Algorithm-Development Pathway and Human-Scorer Dependency Scaling
levers.consequences: Building a semi-automated event-triage tool requires dual human-scorer annotation of all candidate events, which is labor-intensive and introduces bottleneck risk as the dataset grows. Earlier algorithm maturation reduces future annotation burden but demands upfront computational and research-scientist time; delaying development preserves resources for data collection but defers efficiency gains and risks operational gridlock if event volume exceeds annotation capacity.
options: Adopt a conservative iterative approach: collect raw data from the first 20–25 participants without algorithm development, ensure robust dual-scoring infrastructure and inter-rater reliability metrics are established, then train a rule-based event-triage system on a manually curated gold-standard subset (approximately 200–300 events), validating against held-out dual-scored data before deployment in month 18.

Establish a parallel development track from enrollment month 1 by assigning the postdoctoral neuroscientist to build a machine-learning candidate-ranking model using preliminary sensor data and technician flags, iteratively refining it with dual-scored ground truth as it accumulates, and releasing a beta version for technician pre-screening by month 12 with explicit uncertainty quantification and human-override logging.

Commission an external academic collaborator (university signal-processing group or sleep neuroscience research institute) to develop the triage algorithm independently using de-identified data snapshots transmitted quarterly, maintaining separation between algorithm design and facility operations, and licensing the final tool back to the facility under a non-exclusive research agreement, reducing internal cognitive burden but introducing external dependency and potential publication-timeline friction.
levers.review: Controls balance between algorithm maturity (needed to prevent annotation bottlenecks) and data-collection purity (dual-scored ground truth required for algorithm training and validation). Weakness: The options do not establish acceptance criteria for what algorithm sensitivity and specificity would justify moving from mandatory dual-scoring to single-scorer triage review, potentially creating indefinite dual-scoring obligations.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Trigger Thresholds and Capacity Planning
levers.consequences: Expanding from 8 to 12 suites requires capital investment (estimated €200–300K for renovation and equipment) and increases permanent staffing costs; premature expansion before the pilot proves safe operations and acceptable data yield wastes resources, while delaying expansion beyond demonstrated demand leaves enrollment bottlenecked and learning plateaued. The expansion gate (month 24 conditional progress) creates a decisive inflection point.
options: Establish a two-threshold expansion protocol: if by month 18 the facility achieves 25+ enrolled participants with at least 40 adjudicated events and zero serious safety incidents, automatically greenlight €250K of the year-two contingency budget for the four additional suites, with hiring of one additional night technician and renovation completion by month 24.

Maintain the 8-suite baseline through month 24 and use performance data to retrospectively justify a phase-two grant proposal (due month 30) for standalone funding of suite expansion, decoupling expansion from the pilot budget and allowing external reviewers (DFG panels, institutional review) to validate the safety and scientific case before capital commitment.

Introduce a dynamic leasing arrangement with the property owner for an adjacent wing of the residential building, negotiating an option to activate four additional suites on 60-day notice if enrollment demand exceeds supply; retain the flexibility to scale without upfront capital while maintaining a contractual commitment to staff and equipment if utilization justifies it.
levers.review: Controls tension between financial prudence (avoiding premature expansion) and operational responsiveness (avoiding artificial enrollment ceilings). Weakness: The options do not specify which metrics (participant recruitment rate, event-yield percentage, publication output, or safety record) are most determinative of expansion readiness, nor do they address reputational or training-cycle costs of a long delay between pilot and expansion.

levers.name: Matched-Control Recruitment Stringency and Cohort-Composition Trade-offs
levers.consequences: A strictly age- and sex-matched control group provides the cleanest benchmarking for the event-triage tool and baseline sensor patterns, but recruitment of healthy sleepers with documented absence of parasomnias is slower and more resource-intensive than parasomniac recruitment. Relaxing matching criteria or sampling healthy volunteers from sleep clinic visitors accelerates enrollment but introduces confounding (e.g., undetected subclinical sleep pathology, medication use, different sleep architecture) that weakens triage-algorithm validation.
options: Recruit matched controls exclusively from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic's population of non-parasomnia patients (e.g., those evaluated for insomnia, circadian disorders, or sleep apnea screening who are confirmed parasomniac-free), using clinic waiting lists and referrals to stratify by age and sex; prioritize cluster-matched pairs (each parasomnia participant paired with one control within 3 years of age and same sex) to ensure statistical power for tool benchmarking.

Source healthy control volunteers from a community-based register maintained by Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin members, offering the same €80 per-night compensation and 2–4 week stays; relax exact matching to broad age bands (18–35, 36–50, 51–65) and accept a slight imbalance in sex distribution if it accelerates enrollment and reduces study timeline risk.

Conduct an enriched control screening: advertise for healthy volunteers, administer a preliminary telephone-based sleep history interview and actigraphy pre-screening (2 weeks of home actigraphy to confirm absence of nocturnal movement abnormalities), and enroll only those who pass actigraphy scrutiny, achieving a higher-confidence control population at the cost of screening overhead and potential volunteer attrition between telephone and baseline phases.
levers.review: Controls rigor of control-group characterization against enrollment logistics. Weakness: The options do not define what constitutes sufficient control-group size (is it 1:1 paired, or can it be a smaller healthy-baseline cohort pooled across all parasomniac participants?), nor do they address whether differential medication use or sleep history in hospital-clinic-recruited controls introduces unmeasured confounding that must be documented.

levers.name: Escalation-Night Protocol Frequency and Technician Workload Balance
levers.consequences: Triggering full PSG escalation nights (with all-channel recording) after technician-flagged events captures clinical-grade detail but demands real-time technician alertness, setup time, and participant sleep disruption if escalation is frequent. Tightening escalation criteria conserves technician hours and preserves participant sleep continuity but risks missing contextual physiology (e.g., preceding arousals, muscle tone changes) that explains event morphology.
options: Define escalation as technician-triggered in real time only when an event is in progress or imminent (flagged by video-camera motion detection and mattress-sensor pressure anomaly co-occurrence within 10 seconds), with PSG setup initiated within 2 minutes, capping escalations to an average of two per participant per stay; establish a rule that if escalation is triggered more than three times in a single night, the technician documents context (crowded schedule, equipment sensitivity drift, excessive false positives) and convenes a weekly review meeting with the PI to recalibrate detection thresholds.

Implement a scheduled escalation strategy: designate escalation nights in advance (e.g., nights 2, 4, 6, and 8 of each stay) where full PSG is pre-configured and standing, independent of real-time event detection; this reduces participant disruption from mid-event setup and allows technicians to prepare atmosphere and positioning in advance, at the cost of running full PSG on nights with potentially zero events.

Adopt a two-stage escalation model: minor escalations (unanticipated mattress + video motion concordance) trigger enhanced-montage recording (dry scalp EEG + bilateral leg EMG only, no EOG or chin EMG) using cable sets pre-positioned in the suite, reserving full PSG escalation (all channels) only for events associated with technician-observed injury risk or participant call-bell activation, reducing setup burden while preserving physiological context for high-priority events.
levers.review: Controls tension between physiology richness (full escalation detail) and technician sustainability (workload and sleep fragmentation). Weakness: The options do not account for the cost and training overhead of maintaining multiple PSG montage configurations in active suites, nor do they define how technicians assess injury risk in real time while managing multiple suites simultaneously.

levers.name: Publication Sequencing and Grant-Cycle Synchronization
levers.consequences: Publishing methods-validation findings early (month 18–24) establishes scientific credibility and supports the phase-two grant proposal but requires preliminary dataset closure and risks narrative fragmentation if longitudinal phenotyping results lag. Delaying publication until comprehensive 36-month data is available yields stronger phenotyping claims but defers scientific impact and may miss funding cycles, potentially weakening competitiveness for follow-up grants.
options: Execute a rapid methods-first publication strategy: submit a methods paper (facility description, safety protocol, sensor validation, inter-rater reliability, event-capture efficiency in the first 25 participants) to a high-impact journal (e.g., Sleep, Journal of Neuroscience Methods) by month 15, followed by a second phenotyping manuscript at month 30 covering longitudinal episode characteristics in 50+ participants, allowing the first publication to establish credibility for the month 24 interim success review and DFG progress reporting.

Coordinate publication with grant cycles: hold all primary manuscripts until month 28, then simultaneously submit a combined methods-and-phenotyping paper to a sleep medicine journal and a phase-two research proposal to DFG, allowing reviewers to assess the complete dataset and facility maturation without fragmentary preliminary reports; accept the risk that early feedback is delayed and the facility timeline is less visible to the research community.

Pursue a dual-track dissemination: prepare a pre-print manuscript describing methods, preliminary safety data, and sensor benchmarking (published on medRxiv by month 12 for community feedback and citation), then submit a peer-reviewed journal version of the full methods paper by month 18, allowing the pre-print to build scientific narrative momentum while the formal publication establishes citable rigor for grant applications.
levers.review: Controls tension between early scientific visibility and comprehensive data maturation. Weakness: The options do not specify which journals or funding deadlines (DFG cycles, pharmaceutical partnerships, Horizon Europe schemes) are most strategically aligned with publication timing, nor do they address whether early pre-print or conference presentation could fragment the narrative and dilute impact of the final journal submission.

levers.name: Data Retention Duration and Repository Transition Timing
levers.consequences: Storing raw physiological data locally on the facility NAS for the full 3-year duration keeps data under immediate institutional control and simplifies real-time access during research operations, but requires sustained backup infrastructure and electrical costs, and delays transfer to a permanent public repository where long-term preservation is guaranteed. Early migration to a repository (e.g., OpenNeuro, EEGLAB repository) enhances scientific openness but may trigger de-identification workflows prematurely and reduces flexibility for post-hoc data corrections or re-annotation.
options: Implement a phased handoff: maintain complete raw data on-site through month 30 while simultaneously preparing de-identified BIDS-formatted snapshots (updated quarterly) for upload to OpenNeuro or a domain-specific repository; transition the full final dataset to permanent repository custody by month 32, retaining a local archive copy for reference but shifting long-term preservation responsibility to the institutional repository.

Establish a tiered retention schedule: store current and recent data (participants in active enrollment or within 6 months of discharge) on the local NAS with nightly backup; archive data older than 6 months to university cold storage with quarterly warm-storage restoration windows for re-analysis or scoring updates; designate month 36 as the permanent-repository submission deadline, ensuring all de-identification and BIDS validation is complete before handoff.

Negotiate a repository partnership from project onset: engage OpenNeuro or the Max Planck Institute's Data Commons (MPI-M) as a collaborating organization, uploading participant consent forms (modified to include long-term repository deposit) and establishing a shared data-governance agreement upfront, with the repository receiving raw data in quarterly batches beginning month 12, distributing preservation and quality-assurance responsibility across two institutional sites and reducing on-site storage burden.
levers.review: Controls tension between operational flexibility (keeping data on-site during active research) and long-term preservation assurance (repository custody). Weakness: The options do not specify the cost, time, and staffing overhead of BIDS conversion and de-identification workflows, nor do they address what happens to de-identified data already in the public domain if post-hoc re-annotation or methodological corrections are needed mid-project.

Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
